llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This is a difference-in-differences natural experiment. The 'human-only' condition is software-developing startups in the pre-GitHub Copilot period, and the 'AI' condition is the same type of startups in the post-Copilot period. The treatment is the release of GitHub Copilot (AI coding assistant). The comparison arm is 'other startups' (control group that did not benefit from Copilot).
llm_extracted.notes: This is a difference-in-differences study examining the effect of GitHub Copilot's release on software-developing startups. The primary result shows a 19% reduction in time-to-funding (4-month decrease). The regression coefficient β = -4.793 months is in levels (not log-transformed), representing the DiD estimate. Standard error is not reported in the extraction. The paper reports p < 0.01 significance. The 19% figure appears to be calculated as 4.793/baseline_mean, but the exact baseline mean for the treatment group pre-period is not explicitly provided in the extracted statistics (though Column III mentions a mean of 20.768 months for comparison). Without SE and baseline mean, Python will need to rely on the reported percent change of -19% (negative because time decreased, which is beneficial).
source_quotes: We find that software-developing startups raise their initial funding 19% faster and with 20% fewer employed software developers relative to comparable startups after the release of Copilot.

After the release of GitHub Copilot, software-developing startups experience a four-month decrease in the time-to-funding relative to the control group, implying an economically significant reduction of 19%.

In Column I, the coefficient of the interaction term Developer × Post is large, negative, and significant at the one percent level. Its size (-4.793) suggests that software-developing startups experience an average reduction of about five months to initial funding relative to other startups.
